title: Optical pulse-induced quantum geometric waves in graphene
authors: Luis Fernando Cardenas Castillo, Wei Chen
abstract: We show that, under a short optical pulse, the quantum metric of Bloch states in the momentum-time (kx, ky , t) of graphene becomes dynamic and exhibits a wave-like behavior near Dirac points. This quantum metric wave reflects the Floquet-band structure caused by the pulse, as revealed by solving the time-dependent Schrödinger equation assuming that correlations and out-of-equilibrium effects can be ignored. The momentum and temporal components of the metric have very distinct time dependence that persists even after the pulse has passed. In addition, the pulse also generates a Berry curvature wave that is otherwise absent in static graphene. The time-dependent electron densities in conduction and valence bands also give arise to a Fisher information wave that constitutes part of the quantum metric wave, and is readily measurable by pump-probe experiments.
summary: This work theoretically examines how an ultrafast optical pulse induces dynamic changes in the quantum geometry of graphene's electronic states. By calculating the quantum metric and Berry curvature, the authors predict the emergence of measurable 'quantum geometric waves.' These waves serve as a spectroscopic signature confirming the transient formation of Floquet bands in the material.
main_problem: The study investigates how a short, intense optical pulse dynamically alters the quantum geometry of Bloch states in graphene.
main_result: The quantum metric and Berry curvature exhibit wave-like features (quantum geometric waves) in momentum-time space, which are directly linked to the formation of Floquet bands induced by the driving field.
method: Theoretical calculation using time-dependent Schrödinger equation solutions for a two-band model, quantifying geometry via the quantum geometric tensor and Fisher information matrix.
model_or_system: Graphene monolayer modeled on a honeycomb lattice, driven by an external time-dependent vector potential (optical pump). The analysis is performed in the momentum-time manifold (k, t).
key_observables:
Quantum metric tensor components ($g_{\mu
u}$), Berry curvature ($\Omega_{\mu
u}$), and Fisher information matrix ($I_{\mu
u}$).
important_parameters: Optical pulse parameters include $\hbar\omega_{	ext{pump}} = 950$ meV, pulse duration $	au = 18$ fs.
main_assumptions: The analysis neglects correlations and out-of-equilibrium many-body effects by solving the time-dependent Schrödinger equation in momentum space.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate ring-like features (quantum geometric waves) in the momentum profiles of $g_{xx}, g_{tt}, \Omega_{xy}$ at various times, and show similar patterns for the Fisher information components ($I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{tt}$).
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by defining the quantum geometric tensor in the $(\mathbf{k}, t)$ manifold, calculating its real part (metric) and imaginary part (Berry curvature), and introducing the measurable Fisher information matrix. It then applies this framework to graphene driven by an optical pulse.
why_interesting: The connection drawn between time-dependent Floquet band structure, quantum geometric invariants (metric/curvature), and experimentally accessible quantities like the Fisher information makes it highly relevant for understanding non-equilibrium dynamics in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Momentum profile of the elements of the dynamic quantum geometric tensor near the K valley under the influence of an x-polarized optical pulse at the beginning of the pulse t = 50fs, at the peak of the pulse t = 70fs, and after the pulse t = 120fs. The panels show the momentum-space quantum metric gxx/a2, the temporal metric gtt/fs−2, and the Berry curvature Ωxy/a2, all of which manifest ring-like features that respect the Floquet bands labeled by white and black contours.
Figure 1 shows numerical results near the K-valley, where we present the momentum gxx and temporal gtt components of the quantum metric, as well as the momentum component of the

Figure 2: Time dependence of the dynamic quantum geometric tensor along the Γ −K direction caused by an x-polarized pulse, evaluated at below |k(q)| = 1.5754Å−1, exactly at |k(q)| = 1.6196Å−1, and above |k(q)| = 1.7005Å−1 the first resonant condition Ec(k) −Ev(k) = ℏωpump. We present the momentum component of the quantum metric {gxx, gxy, gyy} (first row), temporal component of the metric gtt (second row), mixed component of the metric {gxt, gyt} (third row), momentum component of the Berry curvature Ωxy (fourth row), and mixed component of the curvature {Ωxt, Ωyt} (fifth row).

Figure 3: Momentum profile of the dynamic Fisher information matrix near the K valley caused by x-polarized pulse evaluated at time t = 50, 70, 120 fs. We present the momentum components Ixx and Iyy in units of a2, and the temporal component Itt in units of fs−2. The white contours label the location of Floquet bands.

Figure 4: Time-varying band populations  |c1|2, |c2|2 (first row), momentum components {Ixx, Ixy, Iyy} (second row), temporal component Itt (third row), and mixed component {Ixt, Iyt} of the dynamic Fisher information matrix along the Γ −K direction caused by x-polarized pulse. The columns correspond to momenta below, at, and above the first resonant condition (see caption of Fig. 2).
